coredata.dc:title: Modification-free carbon dots as turn-on fluorescence probe for detection of organophosphorus pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Bixia
Yan, Yun
Guo, Manli
Cao, Yujuan
Yu, Ying
Zhang, Tingyun
Huang, Yan
Wu, Duo
coredata.dc:description: It is important to detect pesticides residues due to the concern over food safety. In this work, the burning ash of waste paper was used as carbon source to synthesize carbon dots (C-dots). The fluorescence of obtained C-dots could been turn off by Fe3+ which was from Fe2+ oxidized by H2O2, organophosphorus pesticides could effectively inhibit the production of H2O2 by destroying the acetylcholinesterase activity, so the fluorescence of C-dots hold turning on in the presence of organophosphorus pesticides. Based on above principle that the fluorescence intensity of C-dots was proportional to the pesticides concentration, take chlorpyrifos for example, a universal method for pesticides detection was established. The linear range was 0.01–1.0 μg/mL with detection limit of 3 ng/mL. The method was reliable and sensitive to actual samples. The imaging of chlorpyrifos on cabbages leaves indicated this method could be used for visualization detection of organophosphorus pesticides in vegetables.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Modification-free carbon dots
Organophosphorus pesticides
Universal detection method
Imaging of vegetables

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: “Turn – off” fluorescence of C-dots by Fe3+
“Turn – on” fluorescence of C-dots by Ops

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of reaction conditions on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots: reaction temperature (a), reaction time (b), the amount of waste paper ash (c) and the amount of sodium hydroxide (d) in 60 mL doubly distilled water.

Characterizations of the as-synthesized C-dots: (a) the low (scale bar is 20 nm), the high-magnified (scale bar is 2 nm) TEM images and the diameter distribution of the as-synthesized C-dots (inset); (b) the XPS spectrum, (c) the XRD pattern, (d) the FTIR spectrum; (e) Uv–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (λex = 320 nm) of C-dots, inset: optical images under daylight (left) and UV light (right); (f) Emission spectra of C-dots at different excitation wavelengths from 250 to 500 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The effect of various metal ions (the concentration was 100 μM) on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots (a); the hydrate particle sizes of C-dots in the absence (1) and presence (2) of Fe3+ (b); the effect of chlorpyrifos, Fe2+, H2O2, Fe3+, the coexistence of H2O2 and Fe2+ on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots (c); the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Fe3+ (d).

The effects of different concentration of chlorpyrifos on the fluorescence of C-dots (a); the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of C-dots and the logarithm of chlorpyrifos concentration (b).

The fluorescence microscopy images of cabbages leaves in blue channel under the same magnification. Leaf was added with C-dots probe (a), leaves were added with C-dots probe, AChE, ACh, ChOx and different concentrations of chlorpyrifos (μg/mL): 0 (b), 0.05 (c), 0.1 (d), 0.5 (e), 1.0 (f). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The detection principle of organophosphorus pesticides based on modification-free C-dots.

head.ce:title: Modification-free carbon dots as turn-on fluorescence probe for detection of organophosphorus pesticides

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environment, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No. 378 Waihuan West Road, University City, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510006, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is important to detect pesticides residues due to the concern over food safety. In this work, the burning ash of waste paper was used as carbon source to synthesize carbon dots (C-dots). The fluorescence of obtained C-dots could been turn off by Fe3+ which was from Fe2+ oxidized by H2O2, organophosphorus pesticides could effectively inhibit the production of H2O2 by destroying the acetylcholinesterase activity, so the fluorescence of C-dots hold turning on in the presence of organophosphorus pesticides. Based on above principle that the fluorescence intensity of C-dots was proportional to the pesticides concentration, take chlorpyrifos for example, a universal method for pesticides detection was established. The linear range was 0.01–1.0 μg/mL with detection limit of 3 ng/mL. The method was reliable and sensitive to actual samples. The imaging of chlorpyrifos on cabbages leaves indicated this method could be used for visualization detection of organophosphorus pesticides in vegetables.

ce:section.ce:para: Some organophosphorus pesticides (Ops) are toxic but still widely used for pest insect control, which causes serious concerns over food safety and environmental pollution (Deng et al., 2015). Thus it is very necessary to detect pesticides residues. Traditional detection methods include chromatography, mass spectrometry (Vicent, Francisco, Ana, & Yolanda, 2014) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays. However these methods involve complex and time-consuming sample preparation. A simple and fast detection method for pesticide residues is needful especially for the on-site analysis of food safety.

During the past decades, fluorescent quantum dots (QDs) have attracted considerable interests (Fereshte, Morteza, Hossein, Mehdi, & Mohammad, 2017). However, the traditional semiconductor QDs are developed from the compounds such as CdSe, PbS or other heavy metal constituents, thus their practical applications suffer from the problems of biotoxicity, environmental concerns and high cost (Pradhan, Battaglia, Liu, & Peng, 2007). Carbon dots (C-dots) are a class of the most popular nano carbon family, which follow the fullerenes, carbon nanotubes and graphene. Due to their fluorescence with adjustable characteristic, stable photoluminescent properties, high chemical durability, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and easy surface functionalization (Huang et al., 2014; Marquis, Love, Braun, & Haynes, 2009; Yang et al., 2009), C-dots have been widely applied in bio-sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery and photocatalysis (Guo, Wang, Shao, & Jiang, 2013; Lai, Hsiao, Peng, & Chou, 2012; Wang et al., 2013; Wei et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2013). See Scheme 1 .

The reported preparation methods of C-dots include laser ablation (Chen et al., 2004), microwave-assisted pyrolysis (Wang et al., 2011), electrochemical oxidation (Wang, Wu, Periasamy, & Chang, 2014) and organic carbon (Muthakarn, Sano, Charinpanitkul, Tanthapanichakoon, & Kanki, 2006). These methods are involved in expensive instruments, extreme synthesis conditions and complex separation. In recent years, except graphite (Zhao et al., 2008) and activated carbon (Wang, Chen, Lu, Liu, & Yang, 2015), some natural materials such as gelatin, soot, silk, hair have been reported as the carbon source for C-dots (Ban et al., 2013; Tian et al., 2009; Wu et al., 2013). The cellulose, lignin and other major elements of the burning ash are the available carbon sources and promising materials for the synthesis of C-dots. Herein, C-dots are prepared by using available waste paper as the major raw material. The burning ash of waste paper is used as carbon source and no other chemical reagents are used except the distilled water and sodium hydroxide in the synthesis, which fully embodies a circular utilization of resources. Neither the expensive and unusual equipment such as electric shock or laser, nor the professional operation is required.

The obtained C-dots have perfect dispersibility and hydrophilicity. Further, it is found that the fluorescence of the modification-free C-dots can be quenched only by Fe3+ derived from the oxidation of Fe2+ by hydrogen peroxide. Acetylcholine (ACh) can be decomposed to hydrogen peroxide by acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (ChOx) under suitable conditions (Liu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2013). Organophosphorus pesticides (Ops) can effectively destroy the activity of AChE and inhibit the production of hydrogen peroxide (Chen, Du, & Lin, 2012; Liu et al., 2012). Thus the fluorescence of C-dots hold turning on in the presence of organophosphorus pesticides. According to the relationship that the fluorescence intensity of C-dots enhances in response to the increase of Ops, a universal method for the measurement of various Ops is established with modification-free C-dots as the fluorescent probes (in Sheme), and chlorpyrifos serves as a model of Ops. This method has the advantages of wide detection range and low detection limit. Besides, the method has potential application for the visual detection, which will facilitate the qualitative or semi quantitative detection of pesticides in daily food safety inspection.

NaOH (AR), H2O2 (30%), (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O (AR), citric acid (AR), Na2HPO4 (AR), quinine sulfate (AR) and chlorpyrifos (AR) were purchased from the Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetylcholine chloride (AR), cholinesterase (BR) and choline oxidase (BR) were purchased from Yike Biotech Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), ribonucleic acid (RNA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), proline (Pro) and pepsin were purchased from Ribio Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Waste paper was the newspaper subscribed by our laboratory. Doubly distilled water was used throughout.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected with a JEM-2100HR (JEOL, Japan) instrument operated at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained with an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK). Powder X-ray diffraction pattern was analyzed using a German Bruker D8 ADVANCE XRD with 2θ scanning mode. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired over the range of 500–4000 cm−1 with an IR Prestige-21 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using a UV–Vis 1700 spectrophotometer (Tianmei, Shanghai, China). Hydrated particle sizes were measured using a Zetasizer Nano ZS Malvin (UK). Fluorescence spectra were measured using F-2500 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) with voltage of 400 V, slit of 5 nm and scanning speed of 300 nm/min. Fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed using a FLS-920 combined fluorescence lifetime and steady-state spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The imaging of leaves were obtained with an LSM 510 Meta Duo Scan laser scanning confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany). The light of 365 nm from an ultraviolet lamp (12 W, WFH-203, Shanghai City Jingke Instrument Co., Ltd., China) was used to irradiate C-dots.

C-dots were synthesized using waste paper through a simple and convenient hydrothermal method. Briefly, the black ash from sufficient combustion of newspapers was collected and grinded fully into fine particles in a mortar. The mixture consisting of the fine particles (1.0 g), NaOH (0.06 g) and doubly distilled water (60 mL) was sealed in a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 15 h. Then the reactant was automatically cooled to room temperature. The suspension solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane after the initial separation to remove large particles by filter paper. The C-dots obtained were dialyzed at 4 °C with dialysis bag (MWCO: 3500 Da) to remove excess small molecules and ions, and the distilled water was replaced after an interval of 10 min, repeatedly 4 times. Finally, yellow C-dots solution was obtained. The concentration of C-dots was 1.73 g/L.

0.0294 g (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O was dissolved in 40 mL of 1.73 g/L C-dots solution. Then the solution was diluted to 50.0 mL with citric acid and disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer (0.2 M, pH = 4). This was used as the C-dots probe in the subsequent measurements. The concentration of ferrous ions in the probe solution was 1.5 mM.

The experiments of detecting chlorpyrifos were carried out as follows. In a series of colorimetric tubes, 2.0 mL of AChE (1.0 U/mL) and 5.0 mL of chlorpyrifos solution were added. The chlorpyrifos concentrations were 1.0, 0.5, 0.3, 0.1, 0.05, 0.03, 0.01 and 0 μg/mL, respectively. After the mixtures were heated at 37 °C for 10 min 1.0 mL of 0.01 M ACh and 2.0 mL of 0.5 U/mL ChOx were added and maintained at 37 °C. After 20 min. 1.0 mL of each solution above was taken out and diluted to 10.0 mL with the probe solution as measuring solution. The fluorescence spectra of the measuring solutions were recorded under slit of 5 nm, excitation wavelength of 320 nm and voltage of 400 V. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of C-dots and the logarithm of chlorpyrifos concentration was obtained.

To evaluate the practical application of the detection method established in this paper, water samples were investigated. Chlorpyrifos was added into actual samples and the final concentrations of chlorpyrifos in the actual samples were 0.10, 0.50 and 1.00 μg/mL. 5 mL of sample solution was taken out and treated as the chlorpyrifos solution in the above experimental method. Finally the fluorescence intensity was measured. Each sample was tested three times, and the concentration of chlorpyrifos was calculated according to the working curve.

In order to study the imaging of the probe in vegetables, cabbages leaves were fixed on the glass slides, the C-dots probe solutions containing AChE, ACh, ChOx and different concentrations of chlorpyrifos at 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5 and 1.0 μg/mL were added dropwise onto the leaves, then the leaves were observed under a confocal laser scanning microscope.

In order to obtain the C-dots with excellent fluorescent performance, the synthesis conditions were optimized. Firstly, C-dots synthesized at 220 °C had the strongest fluorescence within the temperature range from 180 to 280 °C in the hydrothermal synthesis process (Fig. 1 a). Secondly, the effect of reaction time was studied. The fluorescence intensity increased with the extension of reaction time in the range of 3–15 h, and gradually weakened after 15 h (Fig. 1b). Finally, the effects of the amount of waste paper ash and sodium hydroxide were investigated, and the results showed that 1.0 g waste paper ash (Fig. 1c) and 0.06 g sodium hydroxide (Fig. 1d) mixed in 60 mL doubly distilled water resulted in the strongest fluorescence intensity. In conclusion, the optimized synthesis conditions of C-dots were described as follows: 1.0 g waste paper ash, 0.06 g sodium hydroxide, 60 mL doubly distilled water were mixed, then sealed into a Teflon liner and heated at 220 °C for 15 h.

The as-synthesized C-dots were characterized by various techniques. The morphology and size of as-synthesized C-dots were observed by TEM. It could be clearly seen from Fig. 2 a that the C-dots possessed a nearly spherical shape, and the average diameters of C-dots were about 2–4 nm. The inset in Fig. 2a showed the diameter size distribution obtained by Image J software statistical income from more than 100 particles. XPS spectrum in Fig. 2b showed that the C-dots contained 38.47% oxygen and 5.46% sulfur in addition to carbon. The XRD pattern depicted in Fig. 2c exhibited a weak broad reflection peak centered at about 2θ = 20–30°, which suggested the C-dots had an amorphous carbon phase. Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum of C-dots was shown in Fig. 2d. The broad band between 3600 and 3300 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of OH, the small peaks at 1100 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO, and the strong peak at 1740 cm−1 ascribed to the CO stretching vibration. These revealed that the surfaces of the C-dots were full of hydroxyl and carboxyl. The peaks at 2980, 2940 and 2900 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of CH bonds. In addition, the peak at 900 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CS bond.

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum of C-dots were presented in Fig. 2e. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum showed a shoulder peak around 320 nm which was ascribed to the π-π∗ transition of C O group on the surface of C-dots. The fluorescence spectrum showed an optimal emission peak at 420 nm with excitation wavelength at 320 nm and the full-width-at-half-maximum of 80 nm. From the insets in Fig. 2e, it could be observed that C-dots showed faint yellow color and blue fluorescence under the illumination of daylight lamp and UV light (λ = 365 nm), respectively. To further explore the optical properties, fluorescence spectra excited at various excitation wavelengths from 250 to 500 nm were collected (Fig. 2f). It was noted that the strongest emission peaks shifted from 410 to 540 nm with the increase of excitation wavelength. Thus, the C-dots possessed various fluorescence emissions when they were excited by different excitation wavelengths. Moreover, the quantum yield measured by using quinine sulfate as standard sample was 20% in Fig. S1, which was higher than the reported quantum yield (Wei et al., 2013). The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of C-dots was measured by time-correlated single photon counting method, and the obtained lifetime in Fig. S2 was 14.02 ns which was longer than previous reports (Liang, Ma, Shi, Li, & Yang, 2013). To investigate the stability of C-dots, the fluorescence of C-dots under different conditions was measured in Fig. S3. The experimental results suggested that C-dots owned well properties of photo-stability, salt stability and alka resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn – off” fluorescence of C-dots by Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that some metal ions may quench the fluorescence of probes. When different metal ions with the same concentration (100 μM) were separately added into the C-dots solution, the results showed that only Fe3+ could turn off the fluorescence of C-dots (Fig. 3 a). The reason of fluorescence quenching was that there was coordination interaction between Fe3+ and oxygen atoms on C-dots surface, which was verified through hydrate particle size distributions in Fig. 3b. The hydrate particle size of C-dots in the absence of Fe3+ was about 8 nm, after adding Fe3+ the large aggregates with particle size of 60 nm were formed. The quenching of Fe3+ on C-dots was also confirmed by comparing the effect of chlorpyrifos, Fe2+, H2O2, Fe3+, the coexistence of H2O2 and Fe2+ on the fluorescence of C-dots. As shown in Fig. 3c, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots change little after adding chlorpyrifos, Fe2+ or H2O2, which indicated that the presence of chlorpyrifos, Fe2+ or H2O2 had little effect on the fluorescence of C-dots. However, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots was quenched clearly in the presence of Fe3+. And the coexistence of H2O2 and Fe2+ had the similar quenching effect on C-dots as Fe3+. A good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9847) between the fluorescence intensity change of C-dots and the concentration of Fe3+ in the solution was obtained in the range from 0 to 10.0 μM in Fig. 3d. The regression equation was: F/F0 = 0.956–0.0543C (C: μM), and the detection limit of Fe3+ was 0.03 μM. F0 and F corresponded to the fluorescence intensities of C-dots in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. When the concentration of Fe3+ was higher than 150 μM, the quenching rate of fluorescence intensity could reach 99%. ab

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn – on” fluorescence of C-dots by Ops
ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticide can inhibit the activity of AChE, and reduce the amount of hydrogen peroxide generated from choline. So in the absence of pesticide, the generated H2O2 from choline could oxidize Fe2+ to Fe3+, and the fluorescence of C-dots was turned off by Fe3+. While in the presence of pesticide such as chlorpyrifos, the amount of H2O2 decreased and fluorescence of C-dots increased (Fig. 4 a). When the concentration of Fe2+ was 1.5 mM, a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9966) between the fluorescence intensity ratio of C-dots and the logarithm of chlorpyrifos concentration in the solution was obtained (Fig. 4b). The regression equation was: F/F0 = 2.01 + 0.429lgC (C: μg/mL), and the detection limit was 3 ng/mL. F0 and F corresponded to the fluorescence intensities of C-dots in the absence and presence of chlorpyrifos, respectively.

To evaluate the interference of coexisting ions and biological molecules on the detection of chlorpyrifos in actual samples, samples containing 0.1 μg/mL of chlorpyrifos were added with 100 μM of Ag+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cr3+, 400 μM of Ba2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and 100 μg/mL of BSA, DNA, RNA, l-proline, pepsin respectively and detected. The results in Table S1 showed that the error was less than 5%, indicating the interference of coexisting ions and biological molecules on the fluorescence was not obvious.

Samples spiked with different concentrations of chlorpyrifos were measured by using the proposed method, and the results were shown in Table S2. The recoveries of chlorpyrifos in spiked samples were in the range of 96.0% and 98.6% and the RSD (n = 5) was no more than 5.6%. This meant the detection method was satisfactory and reliable for the determination of actual samples.

Compared with other chlorpyrifos detection methods reported in the last few years in Table S3, except the electrochemistry method, the detection limits of this paper was lowest. The method had high sensitivity and wide detection range.

The fluorescence imaging of chlorpyrifos residues on cabbages leaves was tested, and the results were shown in Fig. 5 . The C-dots probe on the leaf had bright fluorescence (Fig. 5a). As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence of C-dots probe was turned off by Fe3+ derived from the oxidation of Fe2+ by H2O2. The fluorescence intensity enhanced gradually in response to increasing concentration of chlorpyrifos (Fig. 5c-f). This showed the method could be used as the universal visualization determination for Ops residues in vegetables. This method was a important supplement for existing analysis methods of pesticide that only detected the pesticide residues content.

This paper established a circular economy synthesis method of C-dots. The obtained C-dots possessed the features of perfect dispersibility and satisfactory fluorescent stability. The fluorescence of C-dots could be turned off by Fe3+ derived from the oxidation of Fe2+ by H2O2, and H2O2 was effectively inhibited by organophosphorus pesticides. Based on this principle the quantitative detection of chlorpyrifos was carried out. The results of chlorpyrifos detection shown this method had wide linear range and better sensitivity. This method could be used as a universal detection method for all kinds of organophosphorus pesticides, and could also be applied for the detection of the total content of organophosphorus pesticides. Moreover, the imaging of chlorpyrifos residues in vegetables was performed preliminarily. The result showed this method could be used as a visual semiquantitative determination method for Ops residues in vegetables.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575043, 21275056, 51478196, 21605052); the Platform Construction Project of Guangzhou Science Technology and Innovation Commission (No. 15180001); and the cultivation foundation of South China Normal University for young teachers (No. 14 KJ08); the Innovation Project of Graduate School of South China Normal University (No. 20161kxm01).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2017.11.038.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bixia Lin received her master degree in Analytical Chemistry from South China Normal University in 2011. She started her doctoral work under the supervision of Professor Ying Yu at South China Normal University in 2015. Her research interests focus on the design, synthesis and application of fluorescent probes.

Yun Yan received her master degree in Analytical Chemistry from South China Normal University in 2015. She is a graduate student under the supervision of Professor Ying Yu in South China Normal University. Her research interests focus on the synthesis of nanomaterials.

Manli Guo is currently a Professor of Analytical Chemistry at South China Normal University. She obtained Ph.D degree (2007) from the Hunan Unversity. Her research interests focus on the design and application of probes.

Yujuan Cao is currently a Professor of Analytical Chemistry at South China Normal University. She obtained Ph.D degree (2005) at the Chinese Academy of Science. Her research interests focus on the design and application of fluorescent probes.

Ying Yu is currently a Professor of Analytical Chemistry at South China Normal University. She received Bachelor degrer (1986), Master degree (1989) from Jiangxi University and Ph.D degree (2005) from South China University of Technology. Her research interests focus on the design, synthesis and application of fluorescent probes.
Tingyun Zhang is an undergraduate under the supervision of Professor Ying Yu in South China Normal University. Her research interests focus on the synthesis of nanomaterials.
Yan Huang is an undergraduate under the supervision of Professor Ying Yu in South China Normal University. Her research interests focus on the application of fluorescent probes.
Duo Wu is an undergraduate under the supervision of Professor Ying Yu in South China Normal University. His research interests focus on the application of fluorescent probes.
